# Tucker Decomposition

## Definition

The Tucker decomposition expresses a tensor as the product of a core tensor
multiplied by a factor matrix along each mode. For a third-order tensor
$\mathcal{T} \in \mathbb{R}^{I_1 \times I_2 \times I_3}$, the Tucker
decomposition takes the form
$$
\mathcal{T} \approx \mathcal{G} \times_1 U^{(1)} \times_2 U^{(2)} \times_3 U^{(3)},
$$
where

- $\mathcal{G} \in \mathbb{R}^{R_1 \times R_2 \times R_3}$ is the **core tensor**,
- $U^{(n)} \in \mathbb{R}^{I_n \times R_n}$, $n = 1, 2, 3$, are the **factor matrices**,
- $\times_n$ denotes the $n$-mode product, and
- $(R_1, R_2, R_3)$ is the **multilinear rank**.

In element-wise notation this reads
$$
\mathcal{T}_{i_1 i_2 i_3}
\approx \sum_{r_1=1}^{R_1} \sum_{r_2=1}^{R_2} \sum_{r_3=1}^{R_3}
g_{r_1 r_2 r_3} \, u^{(1)}_{i_1 r_1} \, u^{(2)}_{i_2 r_2} \, u^{(3)}_{i_3 r_3}.
$$

## Mode-$n$ Unfolding

A key operation underlying many Tucker algorithms is the **mode-$n$
unfolding** (or matricisation) of a tensor. The mode-$n$ unfolding of
$\mathcal{T}$, denoted $T_{(n)}$, rearranges the tensor into a matrix
by mapping mode $n$ to the rows and all remaining modes to the columns.

For a third-order tensor $\mathcal{T} \in \mathbb{R}^{I_1 \times I_2 \times I_3}$:

- $T_{(1)} \in \mathbb{R}^{I_1 \times I_2 I_3}$,
- $T_{(2)} \in \mathbb{R}^{I_2 \times I_1 I_3}$,
- $T_{(3)} \in \mathbb{R}^{I_3 \times I_1 I_2}$.

In terms of the mode-$n$ unfolding, the Tucker decomposition can be written as
$$
T_{(n)} \approx U^{(n)} G_{(n)} \bigl( U^{(n+2)} \otimes U^{(n+1)} \bigr)^\top,
$$
where indices are understood cyclically and $\otimes$ denotes the Khatri–Rao
(column-wise Kronecker) product.

## Special Cases

- When $R_n = I_n$ for all $n$, the decomposition is exact and the core tensor
  $\mathcal{G}$ captures all the information.
- When $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R$ and the core tensor is superdiagonal, the Tucker
  decomposition reduces to the **CP (canonical polyadic) decomposition**.
- The **higher-order SVD (HOSVD)** is a specific Tucker decomposition where
  each factor matrix $U^{(n)}$ is obtained from the SVD of $T_{(n)}$.
